Kotappa Konda

# Kotappa Konda

[](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

![Ratnakar Sadasyula's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!R73I!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F81a2c542-8b3f-4ade-98d2-a40950cffbe3_2640x1980.jpeg)

[Ratnakar Sadasyula](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

Feb 07, 2026

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

4

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Share

Kotappa Konda in Guntur district, is one of the more well known Shaivite temples, the Tirunallu (Jatara) on Shivratri here draws massive crowds. Perched dramatically on a tri-peaked hill, often called “Trikoota,” the temple’s geography itself evokes cosmic themes central to Shaiva philosophy: creation, preservation, and dissolution.

The main shrine dedicated to Sri Trikoteswara Swamy pulses with centuries of devotion. It’s said that the temple’s name and its triple peaks represent the trimurtis—Brahma, Vishnu, and Shiva—with Shiva presiding at the summit. The temple’s historical mention around 1172 AD ties it to the Kakatiya period, hinting at their role in temple-building that blended power with piety.

During the Maha Shivaratri Tirunallu, the entire hillscape transforms into a living spectacle of devotion. Pilgrims climb barefoot, singing hymns and carrying kavadi in reverence. The festival isn’t just a local gathering—it’s a microcosm of rural spirituality, oral tradition, and generational faith.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!BpV1!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F1d9edeb7-ac26-4fc7-9fdb-2916b037dbf0_639x514.webp)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!BpV1!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F1d9edeb7-ac26-4fc7-9fdb-2916b037dbf0_639x514.webp)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Located close to Narasaraopeta in Guntur district, the Kotappakonda temple received regular grants during Krishnadeva Raya’s time. Surrounded by three peaks, Shiva at the Kotappakonda temple is referred to as Trikooteshwara Swamy.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!_Qex!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F22863b00-06e2-40c3-bb3b-114b7fdb953a_680x432.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!_Qex!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F22863b00-06e2-40c3-bb3b-114b7fdb953a_680x432.jpeg)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

The Trikuta Parvatam or 3 peaks at Kotappa Konda, can be seen from any direction. **Brahma Sikharam**, crowned by the temple of Trikuteshwara Swamy, becomes a cosmic axis—linking terrestrial devotion to celestial order.

**Rudra Sikharam**, the older shrine of Kotayya, offers a deeply personal story of divine migration. The legend of Shiva relocating upon Gollabhama’s plea introduces a devotional intimacy, where bhakti shapes the very presence of the divine.

**Vishnu Sikharam**, with the sacred Paapanase Theertham, adds depth to the inter-trimurti dynamic. Vishnu’s penance to Shiva here speaks to a rich theological layering, echoing humility and cosmic interdependence.

The ascetic manifestation of Shiva as **Dakshinamurthy**—a twelve-year-old sage radiating Brahma Jnana, at the **Trikoota Hills** lends Kotappa Konda an extraordinary sanctity, not merely as a pilgrimage site but as a cosmic classroom where divinity turned guru.

The celibate embodiment of Shiva in this form explains the temple’s unique vow to avoid marriages within its precincts—a rare practice rooted in metaphysical reverence rather than ritual convention. It’s as though the entire landscape has internalized his tapasya, transforming the hill into a space of *jnana*, not *samskara*.

This detail also resonates with the **non-dualistic spirit** of Dakshinamurthy, where teaching transcends ritual and becomes direct transmission. The very absence of marriage rituals seems to preserve the sanctified silence and focused austerity of that eternal moment when knowledge was imparted.

As per the Sthala Purana, there was a cowherd Sundudu and his wife Kundiri living at the foot hills of Kotappa Konda. They had a daughter Gollabhama, after whose birth they became prosperous. Gollabhama become an ardent devotee of Shiva, spending time in prayer and meditation, at the old Kotappa temple. In order to test her devotion, Shiva blessed her to be pregnant even though she was unmarried.

However inspite of her pregnancy, Gollabhama did not stop visiting the temple, and kept praying to Shiva as before. Moved by her devotion, Shiva appeared to her, and said that he himself would appear at her home, so that she need not climb the hill daily. However Shiva put in a condition, that while he would follow her, she should not look back even once. However on reaching the Brahma Sikaram, Gollabhama, hearing some strange sounds, looked back, and Shiva immediately became a Lingam.

The place where Shiva turned into a Lingam is the Trikooteswara Swamy temple on the Brahma Shikaram, the older Kotappa temple is still there on the Rudra Shikaram, at a higher altitude.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!tr24!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F59fe935f-4750-4319-9b30-35d85d2e34bd_2048x1537.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!tr24!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F59fe935f-4750-4319-9b30-35d85d2e34bd_2048x1537.jpeg)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!qw10!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F46dc4c4b-ff0b-4383-9e4a-90c617d9166b_550x309.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!qw10!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F46dc4c4b-ff0b-4383-9e4a-90c617d9166b_550x309.jpeg)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Gollabhama herself became one with Shiva, there is a temple dedicated to her there too.

Another story goes that a crow once turned over a pot of curd she used to bring daily to Shiva. Hence Shiva cursed the crows not to come on to the hill, to date you can see no crows there.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!SsA0!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F4969b798-c814-4d5f-8c70-bb55b449e3d5_1024x649.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!SsA0!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F4969b798-c814-4d5f-8c70-bb55b449e3d5_1024x649.jpeg)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Mahashivaratri is celebrated on a grand scale at Kotappakonda. The Tirunallu or Jatra, draws large number of devotees from Guntur and other districts, many seeking blessings of Dakshinamurthy.

These towering, ornate frames—often crafted from bamboo and cloth, painted with vivid mythological motifs—move through the throngs like mobile sanctuaries. Each village competes in artistry and grandeur, and their procession feels like a declaration of local pride woven into sacred rhythm.

Some even liken the Prabhalu to symbolic gateways: thresholds between the mundane and the divine, carried by generations who’ve inherited the rhythm of the festival.

Whether trekking up the steep incline or winding through the newer ghat road, the physical climb to 1,587 feet seems to mirror the inner ascent toward Dakshinamurthy’s wisdom. There’s something sacred in the act of pilgrimage by foot—a way to shed layers of self and arrive open to transformation.

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

4
